content: The eccentric angle of the point $\mathrm{P}(-6,2)$ of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{48}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$ is
explanation:
Co-ordinates of any point on the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$ whose eccentric angle is $\theta$ are ( $\mathrm{a} \cos \theta, \mathrm{b} \sin \theta$ ). $\frac{x^2}{48}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & a^2=48 \Rightarrow a=4 \sqrt{3} \text { and } \\ & b^2=16 \Rightarrow b=4 \\ & x=a \cos \theta, y=b \sin \theta \\ \Rightarrow & -6=4 \sqrt{3} \cos \theta, 2=4 \sin \theta \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{-3}{2 \sqrt{3}}, \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}, \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \theta=150^{\circ} \quad \ldots\left[\cos 150^{\circ}=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}, \sin 150^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2}\right] \end{aligned} $$

content: The tangent to the ellipse $9 x^2+16 y^2=288$ making equal intercepts on the co-ordinate axes intersects the X -axis and the Y -axis in the points $A$ and $B$ respectively. Then $A(\triangle O A B)=$ (where O is origin)
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} & 9 x^2+16 y^2=288 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{32}+\frac{y^2}{18}=1 \\ \therefore \quad & a^2=32, b^2=18 \end{array} $$

Equation of tangents with slope $m$ are

$$ y=\mathrm{m} x \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{~m}^2+\mathrm{b}^2} $$

Since the tangents make equal intercepts on the co-ordinate axes, $\mathrm{m}=-1$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & y=-x \pm \sqrt{32+18} \\ & \Rightarrow y=-x \pm 5 \sqrt{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x+y= \pm 5 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} $$

Putting $y=0$, we get $x= \pm 5 \sqrt{2}$

Putting $x=0$, we get $y= \pm 5 \sqrt{2}$

$$ \begin{array}{lc} \therefore & \mathrm{A} \equiv( \pm 5 \sqrt{2}, 0), \mathrm{B} \equiv(0, \pm 5 \sqrt{2}) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{OA}=5 \sqrt{2}, \mathrm{OB}=5 \sqrt{2} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~A}(\triangle \mathrm{OAB})=\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \sqrt{2} \times 5 \sqrt{2} \\ & =25 \text { sq. units } \end{array} $$

content: The eccentricity of the curve represented by $x=3(\cos t+\sin t), y=4(\cos t-\sin t)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x=3(\cos \mathrm{t}+\sin \mathrm{t}), y=4(\cos \mathrm{t}-\sin \mathrm{t}) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\cos \mathrm{t}+\sin \mathrm{t}, \frac{y}{4}=\cos \mathrm{t}-\sin \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{9}=1+\sin 2 \mathrm{t}, \frac{y^2}{16}=1-\sin 2 \mathrm{t} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{16}=2$

$\Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{18}+\frac{y^2}{32}=1$, which is an ellipse.

Here, $\mathrm{a}^2=18, \mathrm{~b}^2=32$

$$ e=\sqrt{1-\frac{a^2}{b^2}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{16}}=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{4} $$

content: The foci of the conic $25 x^2+16 y^2-150 x=175$ are
explanation:
Given equation of conic is

$$ \begin{aligned} & 25 x^2+16 y^2-150 x=175 \\ & \Rightarrow 25\left(x^2-6 x\right)+16 y^2=175 \\ & \Rightarrow 25\left(x^2-6 x+9\right)+16 y^2=175+225 \\ & \Rightarrow 25(x-3)^2+16 y^2=400 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{(x-3)^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1 \\ & \mathrm{a}^2=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=4 \\ & \mathrm{~b}^2=25 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~b}=5 \\ & \mathrm{e}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{b}^2-\mathrm{a}^2}}{\mathrm{~b}}=\frac{3}{5} \end{aligned} $$

Centre of ellipse is $(3,0)$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Foci } & =(h, k \pm b e) \\ & =(3,0 \pm 3) \end{aligned} $$

content: AOB is the positive quadrant of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$ in which $\mathrm{OA}=5, \mathrm{OB}=3$. The area between the arc AB and the chord AB of the ellipse in sq. units is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Total area of ellipse } & =\pi \mathrm{ab} \\ & =\pi(5)(3) \\ & =15 \pi \text { sq. units } \\ \text { Area of a quarter ellipse } & =\frac{1}{4}(15 \pi)=\frac{15 \pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

Now,

$$ \begin{aligned}Area \,\,of\,\, \Delta \mathrm{AOB} & =\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OA} \times \mathrm{OB} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 3=\frac{15}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Required area

$=$ Area of a quarter ellipse - Area of $\triangle \mathrm{AOB}$

$=\frac{15 \pi}{4}-\frac{15}{2}$

$=\frac{15}{4}(\pi-2)$ sq. units

content: The equations of two ellipses are $\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{2}=1$ and $\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$. If the product of their eccentricities is $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}$, then the product of the length of the major axis and minor axis of the second ellipse is
explanation:
Equation of the first ellipse is $\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{2}=1$

Here, $a^2=4, b^2=2$

$$ \therefore \quad e_1=\sqrt{1-\frac{2}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{4}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

Equation of the second ellipse is $\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore e_2=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{36}} \\ \therefore \quad e_1 \times e_2=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{36}} & \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{36}} & \\ & \ldots\left[e_1 \times e_2=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3} \text { (given) }\right] \\ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{3}=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{36}} & \end{array} $$

Squaring on both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{4}{9}=1-\frac{b^2}{36} \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=36 \times \frac{5}{9} \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=20 \end{aligned} $$

Length of the major axis of second ellipse

$$ =2 \mathrm{a}=2 \times 6=12 $$

Length of the minor axis of second ellipse

$$ =2 b=2 \times \sqrt{20}=4 \sqrt{5} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Product of the lengths of major axis and minor

$$ \text { axis }=12 \times 4 \sqrt{5}=48 \sqrt{5} $$

content: The eccentricity of the ellipse $9 x^2+5 y^2-30 y=0$ is
explanation:
Step 1: Rearrange the Equation

The given ellipse equation is $9x^2 + 5y^2 - 30y = 0$.

Move $-30y$ to the other side: $9x^2 + 5y^2 = 30y$.

Rewrite as $9x^2 + 5y^2 - 30y = 0$.

Step 2: Complete the Square for $y$

Focus on $5y^2 - 30y$. Take out $5$: $5(y^2 - 6y)$.

Complete the square inside the bracket: $y^2 - 6y = (y-3)^2 - 9$.

So, $5(y^2 - 6y) = 5((y-3)^2 - 9) = 5(y-3)^2 - 45$.

Step 3: Write the Equation in Standard Form

Substitute back: $9x^2 + 5(y-3)^2 - 45 = 0$.

Move $-45$ to the other side: $9x^2 + 5(y-3)^2 = 45$.

Divide both sides by 45: $\frac{9x^2}{45} + \frac{5(y-3)^2}{45} = 1$.

Simplify: $\frac{x^2}{5} + \frac{(y-3)^2}{9} = 1$.

Step 4: Identify $a^2$ and $b^2$

Compare with the ellipse standard form $\frac{x^2}{a^2} + \frac{(y-k)^2}{b^2} = 1$.

Here, $a^2 = 5$ and $b^2 = 9$.

Step 5: Find the Eccentricity

The formula for ellipse eccentricity is $e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{a^2}{b^2}}$ (since $b^2 > a^2$).

Substitute values: $e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{5}{9}}$.

Calculate: $1 - \frac{5}{9} = \frac{4}{9}$.

So, $e = \sqrt{\frac{4}{9}} = \frac{2}{3}$.

content: An ellipse has OB as semi-minor axis, S and $\mathrm{S}^{\prime}$ are foci and angle SBS' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &S=(a e, 0), S^{\prime}=(-a e, 0) \text { and } b=a e\\ &\text { Now, } \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{e}^2=\mathrm{a}^2\left(1-\mathrm{e}^2\right)\\ &\Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$

content: A rectangle of maximum area is inscribed in an ellipse $$\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$$, then its dimensions are
explanation:
Refer figure.

Length of rectangle $$=10 \cos \theta$$ and breath of reactangle $$=8 \sin \theta$$.

$$\therefore$$ Area of rectangle $$=(10 \cos \theta)(8 \sin \theta) =40(\sin 2 \theta)$$

Maximum area will occur when $$\sin 2 \theta=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \sin 2 \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \therefore P=\left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \Rightarrow \text { Dimensions of rectangle are } 5\sqrt2,4\sqrt2. \end{aligned}$$

content: The eccentricity of the ellipse $$y^2+4 x^2-12 x+6 y+14=0$$ is
explanation:
Given ellipse,

$$\begin{aligned} & y^2+4 x^2-12 x+6 y+14=0 \\ & 4 x^2-12 x+y^2+6 y+14=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{gathered} 4\left(x^2-3 x+\frac{9}{4}\right)+y^2+6 y+9=-14+9+9 \\ 4\left(x-\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+(y+3)^2=4 \\ \frac{(x-3 / 2)^2}{1}+\frac{(y+3)^2}{4}=1 \end{gathered}$$

Here, $$a^2=1,b^2=4$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & e=\sqrt{1-\frac{a^2}{b^2}} \\ & e=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The length of the latusrectum of an ellipse is $\frac{18}{5}$ and eccentricity is $\frac{4}{5}$, then equation of the ellipse is .....
explanation:
Given, $\frac{2 b^2}{a}=\frac{18}{5} \Rightarrow \frac{b^2}{a}=\frac{9}{5}$

$\Rightarrow \quad b^2=\frac{9}{5} a\quad\text{.... (i)}$

and, $e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}}=\frac{4}{5} \Rightarrow 1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}=\frac{16}{25}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{b^2}{a^2}=1-\frac{16}{25}=\frac{9}{25}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left(\frac{9}{5}\right) a}{a^2}=\frac{9}{25} \Rightarrow \frac{9}{5 a}=\frac{9}{25}$

$\Rightarrow \quad a=5$

$\therefore \quad b^2=\frac{9}{5}(5)=9$ [using Eq. (i)]

$\therefore$ Required equation of ellipse,

$$\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$$
